title: Green ash stool leg
subtitle: A turned and drawknife-shaped leg in green ash
excerpt: A single stool leg in green ash shaped at the shaving horse with a drawknife and spokeshave. Tapers from 32 mm at the tenon shoulder to 22 mm at the foot. The starting project for green-woodwork furniture: teaches taper control and tenon fitting.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the bodger method and the drawknife-to-spokeshave progression. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for the tenon proportions and the wedged-tenon-in-dry-seat joint mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul rather than sawn. Riven blanks follow the grain perfectly.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece; the green-woodwork rough-shaping tool.
A small two-handled plane for shaping curves; flat-sole and round-sole variants.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps a workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.

projectSchedule.title: Shape the stool leg
projectSchedule.body: Rive the billet, drawknife to taper, spokeshave to finish.

projectSchedule.title: Leg is dry and ready to fit
projectSchedule.body: Six weeks drying time. The tenon has shrunk and is ready for a dry-seat joint.

content.text: A stool leg made by the green-woodwork method starts as a
riven
billet
of
green ash
. The riven blank follows the grain, which makes a stronger leg than a sawn one. The
drawknife
at the
shaving horse
takes the blank from a rough split section to a tapered cylinder, and the
spokeshave
refines the surface. Ash is the traditional choice for stool legs: strong, shock-resistant, and it dries hard. A 45 cm billet, 50 mm across, is the starting piece.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Ash is the classic stool-leg wood. It bends without breaking, it is strong along the grain, and the riven surface is clean. Source from a straight-grained ash log, recently felled. The fibres should run straight along the length with no twist. A twist in the billet means the leg will twist as it dries.

Oak and sweet chestnut are harder and equally strong but more difficult to rive cleanly. Hazel makes excellent thinner stretcher rails but is not usually stout enough for load-bearing legs in 50 mm diameters.
Riving the blank

Split the log into quarters using the froe and beetle. Each quarter becomes one leg blank. The blank should be 50 to 55 mm across with the pith at one corner; you will remove the pith section at the shaving horse. See the froe riving technique tutorial for the full riving method.
Drawknife shaping

Clamp the blank in the shaving horse. Use drawknife strokes to remove the outer sapwood and the pith corner, working along the grain. Rotate the blank a quarter-turn after each pass so you are working toward a rough octagonal section. Aim for 40 mm across when the blank is roughly octagonal.

The leg needs to taper: 32 mm at the top (tenon shoulder) and 22 mm at the foot. Work the taper into the drawknife stage. Extend the drawknife strokes further down the blank toward the foot on each pass; shorter strokes at the tenon end preserve the thicker dimension there.
Spokeshave finishing

Switch to the flat spokeshave to refine the faceted drawknife surface into a smooth round cylinder. Push the spokeshave along the blank with the grain, rotating after each pass until the facets are gone. Check the diameter at regular intervals with calipers or a home-made stick gauge.
Shaping the tenon

The top of the leg has a tenon that fits into a drilled hole in the stool seat. The tenon should be 22 mm in diameter and 30 mm long. Reduce the top section with the drawknife, checking frequently against a 22 mm ring gauge. The tenon must be cut from the green leg while the wood is still wet; after drying, it will shrink and fit the hole with a tight dry-tenon-in-dry-seat joint.
Drying

Dry the finished leg in a paper bag at room temperature for six weeks. The leg will shrink slightly across the grain as it dries; the tenon will shrink more than the main body, which is what allows the dry-tenon joint to lock tightly in a dry seat.
Finishing
Raw linseed oil applied after drying seals the leg and slows any further moisture uptake. It is optional on a workshop stool but recommended for a piece that will see damp conditions.
Variations
Stretcher rail: same method, 30 cm long and 22 mm diameter. Fits into holes drilled lower on the legs for lateral bracing.
Oak leg: stronger, heavier, more difficult to rive. Use the same method; oak requires more drawknife force on the roughing passes.

rows.symptom: Leg is not straight after spokeshave finishing
rows.cause: Blank had a slight twist that the drawknife followed
rows.fix: Sight down the leg from the tenon end. If it spirals slightly, the leg can still be used as a chair leg angled outward; a twisted vertical leg is structurally sound. For a perfectly straight leg, select a twist-free blank next time.

rows.symptom: Tenon too large to fit the 22 mm ring gauge
rows.fix: Return to the shaving horse and take lighter drawknife passes on the tenon section only. Check against the ring gauge after each pass.

rows.symptom: Spokeshave digs in and tears the surface
rows.cause: Working against the grain in a section with reversed figuring
rows.fix: Push the spokeshave from the other end of the blank. On ash, the grain usually runs consistently but a small knot or grain deviation can reverse it locally.
